lessons.title: First Day — Greetings & Timings
lessons.scene: It's 8 a.m. in your new apartment in Indiranagar. Lakshmamma, the house help your neighbour recommended, rings the bell for the first time. You open the door.

steps.speaker: Lakshmamma
steps.kannada: Namaskara amma. Naanu Lakshmamma. Pakkad mane Geeta kalsidru.
steps.script: ನಮಸ್ಕಾರ ಅಮ್ಮ. ನಾನು ಲಕ್ಷ್ಮಮ್ಮ. ಪಕ್ಕದ ಮನೆ ಗೀತಾ ಕಳ್ಸಿದ್ರು.
steps.pronunciation: na-mas-KAA-ra am-ma. NAA-nu lakshmamma. PAK-kad ma-ne GEE-ta KAL-sid-ru
steps.english: Hello madam. I'm Lakshmamma. Geeta from the next house sent me.

steps.prompt: Greet her back warmly and invite her in

choices.english: Who are you? What do you want?
choices.hint: Too abrupt, and 'neenu' (informal you) sounds rude to someone older. Use 'banni' (come, polite) to welcome her.
'Beda' means 'I don't want it' — that turns her away! You want to welcome her in.

correctReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
correctReaction.kannada: Dhanyavada amma. Kelsa yaava time beku?
correctReaction.script: ಧನ್ಯವಾದ ಅಮ್ಮ. ಕೆಲಸ ಯಾವ ಟೈಮ್ ಬೇಕು?
correctReaction.pronunciation: DHAN-ya-vaa-da am-ma. KEL-sa YAA-va time BEH-ku
correctReaction.english: Thank you madam. What time do you want the work?

wrongReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
wrongReaction.english: Geeta madam sent me for the work...

steps.grammarTip: Kannada has two 'you': 'neevu' (polite/plural) and 'neenu' (familiar/singular). With elders, strangers and house help, always use the polite forms — the verb ending '-i' as in 'banni', 'maadi', 'heli' carries that respect.

steps.prompt: Tell her you need the work done every morning at 8 o'clock

choices.english: Come every day at 8 o'clock in the morning.

choices.hint: Fixing a clear time avoids confusion. 'Whenever' will lead to missed days. Give a specific time.
'Raatri' means night! For morning work say 'belagge' (morning).

correctReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
correctReaction.kannada: Sari amma, belagge entu gante-ge bartini.
correctReaction.script: ಸರಿ ಅಮ್ಮ, ಬೆಳಗ್ಗೆ ಎಂಟು ಗಂಟೆಗೆ ಬರ್ತೀನಿ.
correctReaction.pronunciation: SA-ri am-ma, be-LAG-ge EN-tu GAN-te-ge BAR-tee-ni
correctReaction.english: Okay madam, I'll come at 8 in the morning.

wrongReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
wrongReaction.english: Tell me at what time, madam.

steps.grammarTip: Add '-ge' to a time to mean 'at that time': 'entu gante-ge' = at eight o'clock. The same '-ge' marks a destination too ('manege' = to the house).

steps.speaker: You
steps.english: Very good. See you tomorrow morning.

lessons.title: Instructions & Asking for Leave
lessons.scene: A week later. Lakshmamma is in the kitchen. You want her to sweep and mop today, and you also need to discuss what to cook. Then she has a request.

steps.prompt: Ask her to sweep and mop the floor

choices.english: Sweep the floor, then mop it.

choices.hint: That asks for dishes, not floor cleaning. 'Nela gudisi' = sweep the floor; 'ottu maadi' = mop.
That's ironing clothes. For the floor you need 'nela' (floor) + 'gudisi/ottu maadi'.

correctReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
correctReaction.english: Okay madam. Shall I wash the vessels too?

wrongReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
wrongReaction.english: Shall I sweep the floor, madam, or not?

steps.grammarTip: To give a polite instruction, take the verb stem and add '-i': gudisu → gudisi (sweep), maadu → maadi (do), tole → toleyiri (wash). Stringing them with 'aamele' (then) is how you list chores naturally.

steps.prompt: Tell her to make rice and rasam for lunch

choices.english: For lunch, make rice and rasam.

choices.hint: That cancels lunch entirely! Name the dishes: 'anna' (rice) and 'saaru' (rasam).
You wanted both. 'Mattu' (and) joins them: 'anna mattu saaru'.

correctReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
correctReaction.english: Done, madam. Shall I add vegetables too?

wrongReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
wrongReaction.kannada: Adru oota-kke enaadru beku alva amma?
wrongReaction.script: ಆದ್ರೂ ಊಟಕ್ಕೆ ಏನಾದ್ರೂ ಬೇಕು ಅಲ್ವಾ ಅಮ್ಮ?
wrongReaction.pronunciation: AAD-ruu OO-tak-ke e-NAAD-ruu BEH-ku al-va am-ma
wrongReaction.english: But you need something for the meal, right madam?

steps.grammarTip: 'Mattu' = 'and' for joining nouns. In fast speech people often drop it ('anna saaru maadi') and just pause. Use 'kooda' (also/too) to add one more thing: 'tarakari kooda'.

steps.speaker: Lakshmamma
steps.kannada: Amma, naale nange rajaa beku. Mommagana habba.
steps.script: ಅಮ್ಮ, ನಾಳೆ ನಂಗೆ ರಜಾ ಬೇಕು. ಮೊಮ್ಮಗನ ಹಬ್ಬ.
steps.pronunciation: am-ma, NAA-le nan-ge RA-jaa BEH-ku. MOM-ma-ga-na HAB-ba
steps.english: Madam, I need leave tomorrow. My grandson's festival.

steps.prompt: Grant the leave kindly and wish her

choices.kannada: Parvagilla, rajaa togoli. Habba chennagi aagli.
choices.script: ಪರವಾಗಿಲ್ಲ, ರಜಾ ತೊಗೊಳಿ. ಹಬ್ಬ ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಆಗ್ಲಿ.
choices.pronunciation: par-VAA-gil-la, RA-jaa to-GO-li. HAB-ba chen-NAA-gi AAG-li
choices.english: No problem, take leave. May the festival go well.
No, I need the work tomorrow.
choices.hint: Technically valid, but refusing a festival leave sours the relationship. A kind 'parvagilla' goes a long way.
She explained clearly — it's her grandson's festival. Just grant the leave warmly.

correctReaction.speaker: Lakshmamma
correctReaction.english: Thank you madam! I'll come the day after.

vocab.english: let it be / may it happen

steps.grammarTip: '-li' on a verb makes a gentle wish or 'let it happen': 'aagli' (let it be), 'baruli' (let them come). It's softer than a command and very common in everyday blessings.
